content.title: Agent Skill Governance
content.summary: A public-safe model for preserving agent and skill knowledge without exposing local prompts or private runtime state.
contentLines: Agent skill governance is the practice of turning repeated agent workflows into durable process assets while keeping local runtime state out of the public archive.
- what validation commands prove it worked;
- how it connects to the wiki and website workflow.
This is enough for future agents and collaborators to understand the operating model.

Raw prompts, memory files, private session notes, unrevised rule packs, account-specific setup, and local tool state should stay in local-only layers. They may inform a rewritten public operating model, but they are not themselves public documentation.
The archive now uses a skill-style approach for local private compilation. The public lesson is not the private source list; it is the repeatable boundary:
- regenerate public data only after gates pass.
content.whyItMattered: It lets the archive preserve agent operating knowledge without publishing raw rules, prompts, or local state.
operationStory: Separate current active rules from historical recovered rules.
Turn repeated workflows into skills only when they are reusable.
Keep sensitive execution context outside the public wiki.
replicationSteps: Document the trigger for a skill.
State what the skill must protect.
Keep commands and validation gates concrete.
Link public skills to public concepts, not local-only state.
failureModes: treating old rules as current policy

content.title: Private To Public Promotion Pipeline
content.summary: The workflow for rewriting local-only archive material into public-safe wiki pages.
contentLines: The promotion pipeline is the repeatable path from local review material to public wiki pages.
1. Index local material without publishing it.
2. Compile local-only review pages that group sources by theme, project, skill, agent, and timeline.
4. Write or update a source note.
5. Rewrite the theme as a compact public page.
6. Link it from the relevant MOC.
7. Run the structure and privacy gates.
8. Regenerate `site-data/` only after the public wiki passes.
- what is the reusable idea or project;
- what can be repeated by another person;
- what was deliberately excluded from public view.

Good promotion turns private evidence into a public lesson. It does not quote raw chats, reveal local setup, or convert every file into a page. One strong concept page is better than many thin summaries.
content.whyItMattered: It gives the archive a repeatable way to grow without turning private review material into public content.
operationStory: Local evidence is indexed first.
Private review pages identify themes and risk.
Public pages are rewritten as source-aware lessons.
replicationSteps: Choose one theme from the private review layer.
Create a public source note or cite an existing one.
Write the smallest durable concept, project, or synthesis page.
Run public structure and privacy gates.
lessons: public writing should explain reusable knowledge

content.title: Public Site Data Boundary
content.summary: The architecture boundary that keeps the website consuming curated public JSON instead of raw Obsidian content.
contentLines: The public site data boundary is the backend contract for the future archive website. It keeps the web layer small, predictable, and safer to deploy.
The frontend can read generated public data. It cannot read raw local sources, local review pages, private reports, or arbitrary Markdown.

In this vault, `site-data/*.json` is the current backend contract. A later server can replace the file transport, but it should keep the same boundary: only curated public fields cross into the website.
- source labels that are safe to show;
- table of contents and related public links;
- operation story, repeatable steps, lessons, and failure modes;
- public timeline or path entries that were written for display.
Public data should not include local-only source locations, review-only queue details, raw transcripts, account state, or unreviewed personal records.
## Why This Is A Backend Decision

Even without a deployed server, the data generator is already acting as a backend. It chooses which pages are visible, which fields are exposed, and which items stay hidden. Treating it as a backend contract now makes the later server simpler.

toc.title: Why This Is A Backend Decision

content.whyItMattered: It prevents the future website from depending on private vault structure or unreviewed Markdown.
operationStory: The public wiki remains the human-editable compiled layer.
The generator turns curated wiki pages into JSON.
The frontend reads generated data, not the private vault.
replicationSteps: Keep frontend inputs under generated site-data files.
Do not expose local-only directories through routes or APIs.
Treat every new public field as a publication surface.
lessons: generated JSON should be narrower than the vault
backend work starts with field boundaries

content.title: Personal Archive Platform
content.summary: A public-safe project page for the personal archive platform that connects the Obsidian wiki layer to future website data.
contentLines: The personal archive platform is the public-facing version of this vault. Its job is to turn learning history, projects, tools, agent work, and reusable methods into a navigable knowledge base and future website.
The platform has four working layers:
- local raw material that stays outside the public graph;
- a local compiled review layer for sensitive or unfinished material;
- `site-data/`, the generated data contract for the website.
The key design choice is that the website does not read the vault directly. It reads curated JSON generated from public wiki pages.
- turning messy personal material into structured source notes and concepts;
- designing a publication workflow with validation gates;
- building a frontend data boundary that can later move behind a server API;
- using agent-readable schema files to keep future maintenance consistent.
The first public scope is not a full autobiography. It is a governed archive of work patterns:
- [[wiki/concepts/private-to-public-promotion-pipeline]] explains how local material becomes public pages.
- [[wiki/concepts/public-site-data-boundary]] defines what the frontend is allowed to consume.
- [[wiki/concepts/agent-skill-governance]] records how agent skills and rules are kept usable without publishing raw prompts.

Local setup details, raw private conversations, account state, unrevised personal records, and secret-like values do not belong in this page or in `site-data/`. They can inform rewritten lessons, but they are not public content.
content.whyItMattered: It turns scattered work history into a governed archive that can feed a website without exposing local-only material.
operationStory: Split raw evidence, private compiled notes, public wiki pages, and frontend data.
Added validation scripts so publishing depends on structure and privacy gates.
Converted the website backend contract into generated site-data instead of direct vault reads.
replicationSteps: Define raw, private, public, and frontend data layers.
Write a source note before promoting claims.
Promote one project or concept at a time through structure and privacy checks.
failureModes: treating private review pages as public sources
letting frontend read the vault directly
lessons: public data needs a narrow contract

Compiled wiki pages may be public when they contain no credentials, raw private logs, local environment maps, personal memory dumps, browser/session data, or true local filesystem paths.
- raw exports from chats, browsers, tools, or local applications
Run structural and privacy checks before committing or pushing.

content.title: Karpathy LLM Wiki Pattern
content.summary: Official pattern source for this vault architecture.
contentLines: The official architecture source for this vault is Andrej Karpathy's `llm-wiki.md` gist:
- raw material remains separate from compiled wiki pages
- the LLM maintains a persistent Markdown artifact
- Obsidian is the browsing and graph surface
- schema/rule files guide future agent maintenance
- `wiki/` is the public compiled layer, not a staging area
- `private-wiki/` can guide future rewrites, but it is not a public source
- durable answers should improve the wiki graph instead of living only in chat
- every promotion should update indexes and `log.md`

content.summary: Secondary GitHub reference for Obsidian vault template and wiki maintenance conventions.
contentLines: This repository is a secondary implementation reference for Obsidian-oriented vault conventions. It does not replace Karpathy's `llm-wiki.md` gist as the official pattern source.
- Borrow only practical vault-template ideas that fit the public-safe structure.
- Keep `_raw/`, private inbox material, and volatile Obsidian state outside Git.
- Validate every publishable page through the local structure and privacy scripts.

content.title: Local Private Compile 2026-05-13
content.summary: Public-safe source record for the local-only private compile pass that informed the next public wiki pages.
contentLines: This source record represents a local-only compile pass used to decide what can be safely rewritten into the public wiki. The underlying material stayed outside the public graph. This page records only the public interpretation boundary.
- The public archive should be a compiled knowledge product, not a dump of local files.
- The strongest public themes are knowledge-base architecture, public data boundaries, agent skill governance, and repeatable promotion workflow.
- Project and capability claims should be rewritten into reusable lessons before they appear in `wiki/`.
- Items that depend on private conversations, local setup details, account state, or unrevised personal records remain out of public pages.

Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern depends on the wiki becoming the durable artifact. For a personal archive, that artifact must also be safe to publish. This means the public layer needs source notes, distilled concepts, and synthesis pages that explain the system without leaking the private evidence behind it.
This page does not make the local-only compile itself a public source. It is a public-safe provenance marker for the rewritten pages created from that review.

content.title: Archive Information Architecture
content.summary: The public information backbone for organizing projects, capabilities, timeline, and review queues before final showcase pages.
contentLines: The archive should become useful as an information system before it becomes polished as a portfolio. This page defines the public information backbone.
- [[wiki/synthesis/project-evidence-matrix]] records project themes and public readiness.
- [[wiki/synthesis/capability-evidence-matrix]] connects capabilities to evidence and repeatable workflows.
- [[wiki/synthesis/public-growth-timeline-framework]] defines how growth and timeline information can be made public without exposing private records.
The highest-value information is not necessarily the newest file. Priority comes from:
- repeated evidence across multiple source groups;
- ability to explain a project or capability publicly;

Each public information page should say what is known, what is inferred, what remains private, and what needs review. This is stricter than a normal portfolio page, but it keeps the archive trustworthy.
content.whyItMattered: It makes information retrievable before it is made presentational.
operationStory: Separated source coverage, project evidence, capability evidence, timeline evidence, and review queues.
Kept sensitive local material in private review layers while creating public navigation pages.
Prioritized evidence relationships over visual showcase polish.
replicationSteps: Start with source coverage.
Group information by project, capability, agent system, and timeline.
Only then write display pages.
lessons: every public claim needs an evidence route

content.title: Capability Evidence Matrix
content.summary: Public-safe matrix linking capabilities to project evidence, repeatable workflows, and future wiki pages.
contentLines: This matrix keeps capability information concrete. A capability should explain what can be delivered, what evidence supports it, and what workflow can be repeated.
| Capability | Evidence route | Repeatable workflow | Public status |
| Knowledge-base compilation | [[wiki/projects/personal-archive-platform]] | raw/private/public/schema split | Published seed |
| Publication safety and privacy gates | [[wiki/concepts/private-to-public-promotion-pipeline]] | scan, review, rewrite, validate | Published seed |
| Frontend data boundary design | [[wiki/concepts/public-site-data-boundary]] | generate curated `site-data` from public pages | Published seed |
| Agent skill governance | [[wiki/concepts/agent-skill-governance]] | promote reusable process into skills | Published seed |
| AI agent system research | [[wiki/topics/ai-agent-systems-moc]] | compare frameworks and extract operating patterns | Needs consolidation |
| Frontend archive implementation | [[wiki/topics/personal-archive-moc]] | build UI from generated data, not raw vault files | Needs project page |
| Local-first data processing | [[wiki/synthesis/archive-information-architecture]] | index metadata, group evidence, promote safe summaries | Needs workflow page |
| Model and learning-loop experimentation | [[wiki/synthesis/project-evidence-matrix]] | separate training lessons from private runtime state | Needs source review |
Every public capability claim should have:
- a public page or source note;
- a project where the capability was exercised;
content.whyItMattered: It turns capability claims into evidence-backed archive structure.
operationStory: Grouped local evidence into capability themes.
Connected each theme to a project and a repeatable workflow.
Avoided generic skill lists without proof.
replicationSteps: Name the capability as an output, not a tool list.
Attach at least one project and one workflow.
Keep private tools and prompts out of the public claim.

content.title: Information Review Queue
content.summary: Public-safe review queue for unresolved information decisions before more archive content is promoted.
contentLines: This queue keeps information quality visible. It is public-safe: it records categories and decisions, not private details.
- Confirm which credential-like findings are live, stale, or false positives.
- Keep rotation actions outside the public wiki.
- Do not publish secret values, account state, or local environment maps.
- Decide which personal details can be public.
- Keep private memories and unrevised personal records local-only.
- Rewrite growth information as project-linked milestones rather than raw biography.
- Choose the first ten public project candidates.
- Require each project to have a goal, evidence route, repeatable lesson, and unfinished status.
- Keep school, account, deployment, and private collaboration context out unless explicitly rewritten.
- Bind each public capability to project evidence.
- Avoid generic claims that cannot be traced.
- Convert workflows into pages before adding broad skill summaries.
- Separate active rules from historical recovered rules.
- Publish operating models, not raw prompts or local runtime state.
- Keep skill creation focused on reusable work.
content.whyItMattered: It makes missing or uncertain information explicit instead of hiding it behind polished pages.
operationStory: Converted private follow-up questions into public-safe review categories.
Kept sensitive values and identities out of the queue.
Defined what must be decided before future public batches.
replicationSteps: Record uncertain information as a queue item.
Classify whether it is security, identity, project, capability, or timeline risk.
Promote only after review changes the state.
lessons: review queues are part of the knowledge base

content.title: Personal Archive Public Roadmap
content.summary: A staged roadmap for growing the public personal archive from safe architecture pages into project and capability pages.
contentLines: This roadmap defines the next public wiki batches. It keeps growth deliberate: the archive should become useful without exposing local-only material or publishing thin pages.
This batch explains how the archive works and how it stays safe.
Next candidates should be rewritten project pages. A good project page needs:
- links to concept or source pages.
Do not promote projects that still depend on unrevised personal context or local setup details.
Capability pages should not be generic skill lists. Each capability should connect to at least one project and one repeatable workflow.
Only after pages are curated should `site-data/` expose them to the website. Display data should remain smaller than the wiki, because not every useful page is ready to be a showcase item.
content.whyItMattered: It turns a large local archive into a staged public writing plan instead of a risky bulk release.
operationStory: Start with architecture and process pages.
Then promote project pages with evidence.
Then add capability pages tied to projects.
replicationSteps: Promote one batch at a time.
Run gates after every batch.
Keep unresolved items in review queues.
lessons: unfinished status is useful when explicit

content.title: Project Evidence Matrix
content.summary: Public-safe matrix of project themes, evidence strength, privacy risk, and next promotion action.
contentLines: This matrix tracks what project information is ready to become public knowledge. It does not expose private source locations or raw local files.
| Project theme | Evidence strength | Public readiness | Next action |
| Personal archive platform | Strong | Published seed page | Expand with screenshots and deployment notes after server plan is stable |
| Private-to-public knowledge pipeline | Strong | Published concept | Add examples from future promoted pages |
| Public site data backend | Strong | Published concept | Convert into API schema when a server is introduced |
| Agent and skill governance | Strong | Published concept | Add public examples of reusable skills |
| Coze/agent builder research | Medium | Needs rewrite | Write a public architecture comparison without local project state |
| Xiaoan/local model learning loop | Medium | Needs source review | Separate public training lessons from private runtime and memory traces |
| AI agent systems research | Strong | Partly public | Consolidate migrated framework notes into cleaner comparison pages |
| Frontend archive website | Strong | Needs integration | Link current frontend implementation to the public data boundary |
| Campus or school-related projects | Medium | Private by default | Publish only generic product lessons after removing identity context |
| Automation and data processing scripts | Strong | Needs curation | Promote repeatable workflows, not local file paths |
- A project page needs a public goal, evidence route, repeatable lesson, and unfinished status.
- A project with personal, school, account, deployment, or credential context stays private until rewritten.
- Assets, screenshots, and generated files are supporting evidence, not the project story by themselves.
content.whyItMattered: It ranks project information by public readiness instead of turning every local trace into a showcase item.
operationStory: Grouped private project evidence into public-safe project themes.
Separated ready architecture themes from sensitive or identity-heavy project traces.
Defined the next project pages as evidence-backed rewrites.
replicationSteps: Choose a project theme.
Check whether its evidence has private identifiers or secret-shaped material.
Write a public page only if the reusable lesson is clear.

content.title: Public Growth Timeline Framework
content.summary: A framework for turning private timeline evidence into public milestones without exposing sensitive personal material.
contentLines: The archive contains many time signals. Not all of them are public growth events. This framework defines how to turn timeline evidence into public milestones.
| Event date | A real project, learning, release, or decision date | Can become a milestone after review |
| Artifact date | A file creation or modification date | Use only as supporting evidence |
| Plan date | A planned task, schedule, or target | Mark as plan, not completed history |
| Reference date | A date quoted inside a source | Verify before treating as an event |
| Uncertain date | Ambiguous or extracted without context | Keep in review queue |
- which project or capability it connects to;
- what the next public artifact should be.
- moving from raw local evidence to reviewed public knowledge.
content.whyItMattered: It preserves growth information without publishing private memories or raw personal records.
operationStory: Separated real events from file modification dates and quoted dates.
Kept private identity details out of the public timeline.
Defined timeline entries as reviewed narrative milestones.
replicationSteps: Classify each date as event, artifact date, plan date, or uncertain reference.
Publish only milestones with clear public value.
Link milestones to projects or capabilities.
failureModes: treating every file date as life history
lessons: public growth should connect to work evidence

library.title: Agent Skill Governance
library.summary: A public-safe model for preserving agent and skill knowledge without exposing local prompts or private runtime state.
contentLines: Agent skill governance is the practice of turning repeated agent workflows into durable process assets while keeping local runtime state out of the public archive.
- what validation commands prove it worked;
- how it connects to the wiki and website workflow.
This is enough for future agents and collaborators to understand the operating model.

Raw prompts, memory files, private session notes, unrevised rule packs, account-specific setup, and local tool state should stay in local-only layers. They may inform a rewritten public operating model, but they are not themselves public documentation.
The archive now uses a skill-style approach for local private compilation. The public lesson is not the private source list; it is the repeatable boundary:
- regenerate public data only after gates pass.
library.whyItMattered: It lets the archive preserve agent operating knowledge without publishing raw rules, prompts, or local state.
operationStory: Separate current active rules from historical recovered rules.
Turn repeated workflows into skills only when they are reusable.
Keep sensitive execution context outside the public wiki.
replicationSteps: Document the trigger for a skill.
State what the skill must protect.
Keep commands and validation gates concrete.
Link public skills to public concepts, not local-only state.
failureModes: treating old rules as current policy

library.title: Private To Public Promotion Pipeline
library.summary: The workflow for rewriting local-only archive material into public-safe wiki pages.
contentLines: The promotion pipeline is the repeatable path from local review material to public wiki pages.
1. Index local material without publishing it.
2. Compile local-only review pages that group sources by theme, project, skill, agent, and timeline.
4. Write or update a source note.
5. Rewrite the theme as a compact public page.
6. Link it from the relevant MOC.
7. Run the structure and privacy gates.
8. Regenerate `site-data/` only after the public wiki passes.
- what is the reusable idea or project;
- what can be repeated by another person;
- what was deliberately excluded from public view.

Good promotion turns private evidence into a public lesson. It does not quote raw chats, reveal local setup, or convert every file into a page. One strong concept page is better than many thin summaries.
library.whyItMattered: It gives the archive a repeatable way to grow without turning private review material into public content.
operationStory: Local evidence is indexed first.
Private review pages identify themes and risk.
Public pages are rewritten as source-aware lessons.
replicationSteps: Choose one theme from the private review layer.
Create a public source note or cite an existing one.
Write the smallest durable concept, project, or synthesis page.
Run public structure and privacy gates.
lessons: public writing should explain reusable knowledge

library.title: Public Site Data Boundary
library.summary: The architecture boundary that keeps the website consuming curated public JSON instead of raw Obsidian content.
contentLines: The public site data boundary is the backend contract for the future archive website. It keeps the web layer small, predictable, and safer to deploy.
The frontend can read generated public data. It cannot read raw local sources, local review pages, private reports, or arbitrary Markdown.

In this vault, `site-data/*.json` is the current backend contract. A later server can replace the file transport, but it should keep the same boundary: only curated public fields cross into the website.
- source labels that are safe to show;
- table of contents and related public links;
- operation story, repeatable steps, lessons, and failure modes;
- public timeline or path entries that were written for display.
Public data should not include local-only source locations, review-only queue details, raw transcripts, account state, or unreviewed personal records.
## Why This Is A Backend Decision

Even without a deployed server, the data generator is already acting as a backend. It chooses which pages are visible, which fields are exposed, and which items stay hidden. Treating it as a backend contract now makes the later server simpler.

toc.title: Why This Is A Backend Decision

library.whyItMattered: It prevents the future website from depending on private vault structure or unreviewed Markdown.
operationStory: The public wiki remains the human-editable compiled layer.
The generator turns curated wiki pages into JSON.
The frontend reads generated data, not the private vault.
replicationSteps: Keep frontend inputs under generated site-data files.
Do not expose local-only directories through routes or APIs.
Treat every new public field as a publication surface.
lessons: generated JSON should be narrower than the vault
backend work starts with field boundaries

contentLines: ## Public By Default Only After Filtering
Compiled wiki pages may be public when they contain no credentials, raw private logs, local environment maps, personal memory dumps, browser/session data, or true local filesystem paths.
- raw exports from chats, browsers, tools, or local applications
Run structural and privacy checks before committing or pushing.

library.title: Karpathy LLM Wiki Pattern
library.summary: Official pattern source for this vault architecture.
contentLines: The official architecture source for this vault is Andrej Karpathy's `llm-wiki.md` gist:
- raw material remains separate from compiled wiki pages
- the LLM maintains a persistent Markdown artifact
- Obsidian is the browsing and graph surface
- schema/rule files guide future agent maintenance
- `wiki/` is the public compiled layer, not a staging area
- `private-wiki/` can guide future rewrites, but it is not a public source
- durable answers should improve the wiki graph instead of living only in chat
- every promotion should update indexes and `log.md`

library.title: llm-wikid
library.summary: Secondary GitHub reference for Obsidian vault template and wiki maintenance conventions.
contentLines: This repository is a secondary implementation reference for Obsidian-oriented vault conventions. It does not replace Karpathy's `llm-wiki.md` gist as the official pattern source.
- Borrow only practical vault-template ideas that fit the public-safe structure.
- Keep `_raw/`, private inbox material, and volatile Obsidian state outside Git.
- Validate every publishable page through the local structure and privacy scripts.

library.title: Local Private Compile 2026-05-13
library.summary: Public-safe source record for the local-only private compile pass that informed the next public wiki pages.
contentLines: This source record represents a local-only compile pass used to decide what can be safely rewritten into the public wiki. The underlying material stayed outside the public graph. This page records only the public interpretation boundary.
- The public archive should be a compiled knowledge product, not a dump of local files.
- The strongest public themes are knowledge-base architecture, public data boundaries, agent skill governance, and repeatable promotion workflow.
- Project and capability claims should be rewritten into reusable lessons before they appear in `wiki/`.
- Items that depend on private conversations, local setup details, account state, or unrevised personal records remain out of public pages.

Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern depends on the wiki becoming the durable artifact. For a personal archive, that artifact must also be safe to publish. This means the public layer needs source notes, distilled concepts, and synthesis pages that explain the system without leaking the private evidence behind it.
This page does not make the local-only compile itself a public source. It is a public-safe provenance marker for the rewritten pages created from that review.

- Do not copy its structure over Karpathy's raw-source to compiled-wiki pattern.

library.title: Archive Information Architecture
library.summary: The public information backbone for organizing projects, capabilities, timeline, and review queues before final showcase pages.
contentLines: The archive should become useful as an information system before it becomes polished as a portfolio. This page defines the public information backbone.
- [[wiki/synthesis/project-evidence-matrix]] records project themes and public readiness.
- [[wiki/synthesis/capability-evidence-matrix]] connects capabilities to evidence and repeatable workflows.
- [[wiki/synthesis/public-growth-timeline-framework]] defines how growth and timeline information can be made public without exposing private records.
The highest-value information is not necessarily the newest file. Priority comes from:
- repeated evidence across multiple source groups;
- ability to explain a project or capability publicly;

Each public information page should say what is known, what is inferred, what remains private, and what needs review. This is stricter than a normal portfolio page, but it keeps the archive trustworthy.
library.whyItMattered: It makes information retrievable before it is made presentational.
operationStory: Separated source coverage, project evidence, capability evidence, timeline evidence, and review queues.
Kept sensitive local material in private review layers while creating public navigation pages.
Prioritized evidence relationships over visual showcase polish.
replicationSteps: Start with source coverage.
Group information by project, capability, agent system, and timeline.
Only then write display pages.
lessons: every public claim needs an evidence route

library.title: Capability Evidence Matrix
library.summary: Public-safe matrix linking capabilities to project evidence, repeatable workflows, and future wiki pages.
contentLines: This matrix keeps capability information concrete. A capability should explain what can be delivered, what evidence supports it, and what workflow can be repeated.
| Capability | Evidence route | Repeatable workflow | Public status |
| Knowledge-base compilation | [[wiki/projects/personal-archive-platform]] | raw/private/public/schema split | Published seed |
| Publication safety and privacy gates | [[wiki/concepts/private-to-public-promotion-pipeline]] | scan, review, rewrite, validate | Published seed |
| Frontend data boundary design | [[wiki/concepts/public-site-data-boundary]] | generate curated `site-data` from public pages | Published seed |
| Agent skill governance | [[wiki/concepts/agent-skill-governance]] | promote reusable process into skills | Published seed |
| AI agent system research | [[wiki/topics/ai-agent-systems-moc]] | compare frameworks and extract operating patterns | Needs consolidation |
| Frontend archive implementation | [[wiki/topics/personal-archive-moc]] | build UI from generated data, not raw vault files | Needs project page |
| Local-first data processing | [[wiki/synthesis/archive-information-architecture]] | index metadata, group evidence, promote safe summaries | Needs workflow page |
| Model and learning-loop experimentation | [[wiki/synthesis/project-evidence-matrix]] | separate training lessons from private runtime state | Needs source review |
Every public capability claim should have:
- a public page or source note;
- a project where the capability was exercised;
library.whyItMattered: It turns capability claims into evidence-backed archive structure.
operationStory: Grouped local evidence into capability themes.
Connected each theme to a project and a repeatable workflow.
Avoided generic skill lists without proof.
replicationSteps: Name the capability as an output, not a tool list.
Attach at least one project and one workflow.
Keep private tools and prompts out of the public claim.

library.title: Information Review Queue
library.summary: Public-safe review queue for unresolved information decisions before more archive content is promoted.
contentLines: This queue keeps information quality visible. It is public-safe: it records categories and decisions, not private details.
- Confirm which credential-like findings are live, stale, or false positives.
- Keep rotation actions outside the public wiki.
- Do not publish secret values, account state, or local environment maps.
- Decide which personal details can be public.
- Keep private memories and unrevised personal records local-only.
- Rewrite growth information as project-linked milestones rather than raw biography.
- Choose the first ten public project candidates.
- Require each project to have a goal, evidence route, repeatable lesson, and unfinished status.
- Keep school, account, deployment, and private collaboration context out unless explicitly rewritten.
- Bind each public capability to project evidence.
- Avoid generic claims that cannot be traced.
- Convert workflows into pages before adding broad skill summaries.
- Separate active rules from historical recovered rules.
- Publish operating models, not raw prompts or local runtime state.
- Keep skill creation focused on reusable work.
library.whyItMattered: It makes missing or uncertain information explicit instead of hiding it behind polished pages.
operationStory: Converted private follow-up questions into public-safe review categories.
Kept sensitive values and identities out of the queue.
Defined what must be decided before future public batches.
replicationSteps: Record uncertain information as a queue item.
Classify whether it is security, identity, project, capability, or timeline risk.
Promote only after review changes the state.
lessons: review queues are part of the knowledge base

library.title: Personal Archive Public Roadmap
library.summary: A staged roadmap for growing the public personal archive from safe architecture pages into project and capability pages.
contentLines: This roadmap defines the next public wiki batches. It keeps growth deliberate: the archive should become useful without exposing local-only material or publishing thin pages.
This batch explains how the archive works and how it stays safe.
Next candidates should be rewritten project pages. A good project page needs:
- links to concept or source pages.
Do not promote projects that still depend on unrevised personal context or local setup details.
Capability pages should not be generic skill lists. Each capability should connect to at least one project and one repeatable workflow.
Only after pages are curated should `site-data/` expose them to the website. Display data should remain smaller than the wiki, because not every useful page is ready to be a showcase item.
library.whyItMattered: It turns a large local archive into a staged public writing plan instead of a risky bulk release.
operationStory: Start with architecture and process pages.
Then promote project pages with evidence.
Then add capability pages tied to projects.
replicationSteps: Promote one batch at a time.
Run gates after every batch.
Keep unresolved items in review queues.
lessons: unfinished status is useful when explicit

library.title: Project Evidence Matrix
library.summary: Public-safe matrix of project themes, evidence strength, privacy risk, and next promotion action.
contentLines: This matrix tracks what project information is ready to become public knowledge. It does not expose private source locations or raw local files.
| Project theme | Evidence strength | Public readiness | Next action |
| Personal archive platform | Strong | Published seed page | Expand with screenshots and deployment notes after server plan is stable |
| Private-to-public knowledge pipeline | Strong | Published concept | Add examples from future promoted pages |
| Public site data backend | Strong | Published concept | Convert into API schema when a server is introduced |
| Agent and skill governance | Strong | Published concept | Add public examples of reusable skills |
| Coze/agent builder research | Medium | Needs rewrite | Write a public architecture comparison without local project state |
| Xiaoan/local model learning loop | Medium | Needs source review | Separate public training lessons from private runtime and memory traces |
| AI agent systems research | Strong | Partly public | Consolidate migrated framework notes into cleaner comparison pages |
| Frontend archive website | Strong | Needs integration | Link current frontend implementation to the public data boundary |
| Campus or school-related projects | Medium | Private by default | Publish only generic product lessons after removing identity context |
| Automation and data processing scripts | Strong | Needs curation | Promote repeatable workflows, not local file paths |
- A project page needs a public goal, evidence route, repeatable lesson, and unfinished status.
- A project with personal, school, account, deployment, or credential context stays private until rewritten.
- Assets, screenshots, and generated files are supporting evidence, not the project story by themselves.
library.whyItMattered: It ranks project information by public readiness instead of turning every local trace into a showcase item.
operationStory: Grouped private project evidence into public-safe project themes.
Separated ready architecture themes from sensitive or identity-heavy project traces.
Defined the next project pages as evidence-backed rewrites.
replicationSteps: Choose a project theme.
Check whether its evidence has private identifiers or secret-shaped material.
Write a public page only if the reusable lesson is clear.

library.title: Public Growth Timeline Framework
library.summary: A framework for turning private timeline evidence into public milestones without exposing sensitive personal material.
contentLines: The archive contains many time signals. Not all of them are public growth events. This framework defines how to turn timeline evidence into public milestones.
| Event date | A real project, learning, release, or decision date | Can become a milestone after review |
| Artifact date | A file creation or modification date | Use only as supporting evidence |
| Plan date | A planned task, schedule, or target | Mark as plan, not completed history |
| Reference date | A date quoted inside a source | Verify before treating as an event |
| Uncertain date | Ambiguous or extracted without context | Keep in review queue |
- which project or capability it connects to;
- what the next public artifact should be.
- moving from raw local evidence to reviewed public knowledge.
library.whyItMattered: It preserves growth information without publishing private memories or raw personal records.
operationStory: Separated real events from file modification dates and quoted dates.
Kept private identity details out of the public timeline.
Defined timeline entries as reviewed narrative milestones.
replicationSteps: Classify each date as event, artifact date, plan date, or uncertain reference.
Publish only milestones with clear public value.
Link milestones to projects or capabilities.
failureModes: treating every file date as life history
lessons: public growth should connect to work evidence

paths.title: An LLM Wiki
paths.summary: A public, privacy-filtered Obsidian LLM Wiki rebuilt around Karpathy's llm-wiki pattern.
contentLines: This is a public-safe personal Obsidian knowledge base rebuilt around Andrej Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern.

works.title: Personal Archive Platform
works.summary: A public-safe project page for the personal archive platform that connects the Obsidian wiki layer to future website data.
contentLines: The personal archive platform is the public-facing version of this vault. Its job is to turn learning history, projects, tools, agent work, and reusable methods into a navigable knowledge base and future website.
The platform has four working layers:
- local raw material that stays outside the public graph;
- a local compiled review layer for sensitive or unfinished material;
- `site-data/`, the generated data contract for the website.
The key design choice is that the website does not read the vault directly. It reads curated JSON generated from public wiki pages.
- turning messy personal material into structured source notes and concepts;
- designing a publication workflow with validation gates;
- building a frontend data boundary that can later move behind a server API;
- using agent-readable schema files to keep future maintenance consistent.
The first public scope is not a full autobiography. It is a governed archive of work patterns:
- [[wiki/concepts/private-to-public-promotion-pipeline]] explains how local material becomes public pages.
- [[wiki/concepts/public-site-data-boundary]] defines what the frontend is allowed to consume.
- [[wiki/concepts/agent-skill-governance]] records how agent skills and rules are kept usable without publishing raw prompts.

Local setup details, raw private conversations, account state, unrevised personal records, and secret-like values do not belong in this page or in `site-data/`. They can inform rewritten lessons, but they are not public content.
works.whyItMattered: It turns scattered work history into a governed archive that can feed a website without exposing local-only material.
operationStory: Split raw evidence, private compiled notes, public wiki pages, and frontend data.
Added validation scripts so publishing depends on structure and privacy gates.
Converted the website backend contract into generated site-data instead of direct vault reads.
replicationSteps: Define raw, private, public, and frontend data layers.
Write a source note before promoting claims.
Promote one project or concept at a time through structure and privacy checks.
failureModes: treating private review pages as public sources
letting frontend read the vault directly
lessons: public data needs a narrow contract
